g.data-narration: Welcome to Process Concept and Scheduling - the foundation of how operating systems manage program execution.
A program is a passive recipe, while a process is an active entity - a program in execution with current activity.
g.text: Like a recipe written on paper

g.data-narration: A process includes program counter, registers, stack for function calls, data section for globals, and heap for dynamic memory.
The Process Control Block (PCB) is the OS data structure that represents each process, containing all necessary information.
g.text: The OS representation of a process - a data structure storing all process information
PCB = { PID, State, PC, Registers, Scheduling, Memory, I/O, ... }

g.data-narration: PCB stores process state, PID, program counter, CPU registers, scheduling info, memory management data, accounting, and I/O status.
A process moves through five states: New (being created), Ready (waiting for CPU), Running (executing), Waiting (for event), Terminated.
Processes transition between states: New to Ready via admission, Ready to Running via dispatch, Running to Waiting on I/O, Waiting to Ready on I/O completion.
OS maintains job queue for all processes, ready queue for CPU-bound processes, and device queues for I/O waiting processes.
Three schedulers exist: Long-term controls degree of multiprogramming, short-term allocates CPU frequently, medium-term handles swapping.
Context switch saves old process state to PCB and loads new process state - pure overhead costing 1-1000 microseconds.
UNIX process creation uses fork() to create a copy, then exec() to replace the child's memory with a new program.
Shared memory IPC maps a memory region into both processes' address spaces - fast but requires synchronization like semaphores.
g.text: ✓ Fast (no kernel after setup) | ⚠ Needs synchronization

g.data-narration: Message passing IPC uses kernel to route messages between processes - slower but simpler to implement without race conditions.
g.text: ✓ Simpler (no sync needed) | ⚠ Slower (system call overhead)

g.data-narration: Key points: PCB stores process state, state transitions drive scheduling, three schedulers manage processes, context switch has overhead.
g.text: • Process states: New → Ready → Running → Waiting → Terminated
• IPC: Shared memory (fast) vs Message passing (simple)

g.data-narration: Remember: PCB components, state transition causes, scheduler differences, context switch cost, fork/exec mechanics, IPC trade-offs.
g.text: ✓ Draw state diagram with transition causes
✓ Know PCB components and why each matters
